messages.content: in github I have main branch, but I already did add master branch with guide, what is the difference between these branches and why default branch is main but all guides say to create master branch? and do I need to keep only one?

In GitHub, the term "main" branch has been gradually replacing the "master" branch as the default branch name. This change is driven by an effort to remove words with potentially negative historical associations and to promote inclusivity.

The main difference between these branches is essentially the name itself. Neither branch has any special functionality or features associated with it.

When starting a new repository, GitHub now suggests using the name "main" for the default branch. However, many guides or existing repositories might still refer to the default branch as "master." You can choose to use either name depending on your preference or the existing conventions in your development community.

You do not necessarily need to keep both branches. If you no longer require the "master" branch, you can delete it. But before doing so, make sure you have made the necessary updates in your repository settings, such as switching the default branch to "main" and updating any relevant links or references in your documentation or CI/CD configurations.
what is merge in github? I know what commit and push does, but how and when do I use merge or some other actions like fork?

In GitHub, merging is the process of integrating changes from one branch into another. It is commonly used to combine changes made in a feature branch back into the main branch (e.g., merging a "feature" branch into the "main" branch).

When to use merge:
- When you have finished working on a feature or bug fix in a separate branch and want to incorporate those changes into the main branch.
- When working collaboratively, other team members may have created different branches for their features. Merging allows you to combine these changes into a single branch.

Here's a step-by-step guide on how to merge branches in GitHub:
1. Ensure that the branch you want to merge into (e.g., main branch) is up to date with the latest changes. You might need to pull the latest changes to your local repository.
2. Navigate to the branch you want to merge into (e.g., main branch) in the GitHub interface.
3. Click on the "Compare & pull request" button.
4. Review the changes proposed in the pull request.
5. If everything looks good, click on the "Merge pull request" button to merge the branches.
6. Optionally, you can add a comment or description summarizing the changes made in the branch.
7. Finally, confirm the merge by clicking on "Confirm merge" or "Merge pull request."

Regarding forks, forking is a way to create a personal copy of someone else's repository on GitHub. It allows you to freely experiment with changes without affecting the original repository. You might use forks when you want to make contributions to someone else's project by creating your own branch, making changes, and then submitting a pull request to propose those changes to the original repository owner. Forks are also helpful when working collaboratively on open source projects.

so does fork in github mean making another branch essentially? and then I can in example fix bugs in that fork and then merge with main branch. or are there any other methods to duplicate code beside from forks when working in team or when fixing bugs?

In GitHub, forking a repository is slightly different from creating another branch. Forking a repository creates an entirely separate copy of the original repository, including its entire history and codebase. It acts as a standalone repository under your account.

When you fork a repository, you can make changes to your forked repository independently without affecting the original repository. You can create branches within your forked repository to work on different features or bug fixes.

If you need to fix bugs or implement new features, you have a few options:

1. Creating a Branch: If you have write access to the repository, you can create a branch directly within the repository. This is the most common approach when working within a team. You can make changes on the branch, test them, and then merge the branch into the main branch after review.

2. Forking: Forking is typically used when you want to contribute changes to another repository. Forking creates a copy of the repository under your account, allowing you to freely make changes. You can create branches within the forked repository, make changes, and then submit a pull request to propose those changes to the original repository.

Depending on the project and your role, you might have different permissions and access levels. If you are part of a team with write access to the repository, creating a branch directly within the repository might be a more efficient approach. Forking is often used when contributing to open-source projects or when you don't have write access to the original repository.

can css styles be overriden? in example when I create a general style in different file and then specify in my html element another style

Yes, CSS styles can be overridden in HTML. When you apply styles to an HTML element, there is a hierarchy of specificity determining which styles take precedence. Inline styles, styles defined in a `
Example Text
``` 3. External Stylesheets: Styles defined in external CSS files linked to the HTML file. External stylesheets have the lowest specificity level and will be overridden by inline styles and internal styles. Example: ```html
Example Text
``` CSS Selectors such as classes, ids, element selectors, and more can be used to define styles with varying specificity. You can use more specific selectors to override styles applied by less specific selectors. For example, if you have a general style defined in an external CSS file and want to override it for a specific HTML element, you can use a more specific selector or add a class or ID selector to override the style. By specifying the element's class or ID in the CSS, you can give the element a different style. Example: ```css /* External CSS file styles.css */ .example { color: red; /* General style */ } #specificExample { color: blue; /* Specific style */ } ``` ```html
General Text
Specific Text
``` In the above example, the "General Text" will have a red color due to the general style defined in the `.example` class. The "Specific Text" will have a blue color as it has a more specific ID selector defined in the CSS.

I have code
<head>
    <link rel="styles" href="styles.css">
<title>Страница</title>
</head>
and then styles.css file in the same location, but styles dont work, why?

The issue might be with the `link` tag's `rel` attribute value. In your code, it is set to "styles", but it should be set to "stylesheet" for linking a CSS file.

Here's the corrected code:

```html
<head>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
    <title>Страница</title>
</head>
```

Make sure that:
1. The `styles.css` file is located in the same directory as the HTML file.
2. The CSS file is named `styles.css` and not `styles.txt`.

If the issue persists even after these changes, check the network tab in your browser's developer tools to see if there are any errors or issues loading the CSS file. Additionally, ensure that the CSS file has the correct syntax and there are no errors within the file itself.

how can I make these link open in new tab?

The code you provided is already set up to open the links in a new tab. The `target="_blank"` attribute in the `<area>` tags specifies that the links should open in a new tab or window.

When a user clicks on the areas defined by the `<area>` elements, the corresponding links will open in new tabs due to the `target="_blank"` attribute.

Here's the relevant code from your example:

```html
<area shape="rect" alt="oshibka1" coords="0,0,250,200" href="https://example.com/link1" target="_blank">
<area shape="rect" alt="oshibka2" coords="250,0,500,200" href="https://example.com/link2" target="_blank">
```

The `target="_blank"` attribute tells the browser to open the specified URLs, "https://example.com/link1" and "https://example.com/link2", in new tabs.

Please note that the behavior of opening in a new tab or window can be controlled by the user's browser settings, and it can vary from one browser to another. However, using `target="_blank"` provides an indication to the browser to attempt opening the link in a new tab or window, following the user's preferences.
